JavaScript Core (69) - ES6: Template Literals - A Quick Intro to Template Literals
Template literals are one of those things you'll end up using all the time in modern JavaScript. So before we go anywhere, let's get a quick feel for what they are.
A quick intro to template literals
Fun bit of history first — template literals used to be called "template strings." The name got changed to "template literals" later on.
So what do they actually do? Let me show you how we used to build strings back in the day:
'，目前經營一個部落格，網址是 '
// 我的名字叫做 Ray，目前經營一個部落格，網址是 https://israynotarray.com/
See how building a string means stitching variables and text together with the plus operator (

)? It's fine when it's short like this. But the moment you're putting together something more complex — say a chunk of DOM markup with line breaks in it — it gets really hard to read and really annoying to write.
With template literals, you can rewrite that as:
}，目前經營一個部落格，網址是 ${
The basic idea is you wrap the whole thing in backticks, and whenever you want to drop a variable in, you use
with the variable inside. That's it. No more plus signs, way easier to read, and inserting variables is dead simple.
doesn't just take variables, it takes expressions. So if the variable you pass in is empty, or you forgot to assign it and it comes back as
to hand it a default value:
// 我的名字叫做 是 Ray 不是 Array，目前經營一個部落格，網址是 https://israynotarray.com/
And just like that, you've got an easy way to set default values.

Template literals are one of those techniques you'll use constantly in modern JavaScript. This post walks through the basics — how they replace the old string-concatenation-with-plus approach, how to drop variables in with ${}, and a handy trick for setting default values when a variable comes back empty or undefined.
